missions.name: Systems Checkout
missions.description: EDL complete. Before ops clears you for field work, the bus needs a once-over.
missions.briefing:
Landing telemetry says you're intact. Landing telemetry is generated by the same firmware that once reported a successful parachute deploy while the chute was still packed. Trust, but verify — starting with the power bus.

Boot the reactor. Check your profile to see what you're working with. Roll the wheels. Prove you can drive in something approximating a straight line. Confirm the heater isn't decorative.

When all that's stamped, pull up the low-gain antenna. That's where your mission queue lives — and the transmit button is how Earth knows you did anything at all. Nothing counts until the paperwork reaches orbit.

One more thing: manifest shows an additional payload module that isn't on the standard packing list. Finish the checkout and we'll clear it for install. Catalogued under 'discretionary science hardware.' Nobody in procurement could explain it either.

SP accrues as you complete objectives. Check the reward track — the rover gets better the more science it does. Think of it as compound interest, except the bank is on another planet.

missions.name: First Light (Position Fix)
missions.description: Consortium baseline MEC-4401: confirm ground coordinates before any instrument time is billed to the program.
missions.briefing:
Checkout stamped. Systems nominal — or nominal enough for government work.

Descent telemetry is filed as nominal. The spreadsheet says you're fine. Mars says spreadsheets lie. Three transponder stakes were emplaced pre-landing — Alpha, Beta, Gamma. Per LVOP checkout you need an echo off each before we can assign a map grid and pretend the insurance paperwork matches reality. Drive the circuit. Ping all three. After that, Section 1 of your onboarding packet is stamped complete. Section 2 is weather. Try to look excited on the inside.

missions.name: RTG — Corrective Maintenance
missions.description: Vehicle age: ~175 years, refurbishment status: "extensive." The RTG disagrees with the brochure.
missions.briefing: Post-shakedown power curve is trending wrong. Not an emergency — emergencies get their own forms. This is deferred maintenance that became urgent because someone dented something during EDL and nobody filed it. You have consumables in-kit; bring the stack back within tolerance or we hold science ops until Engineering stops arguing in the thread. Standard MEC liability language applies. So does rust, probably.

missions.name: REMS — Environmental Baseline
missions.description: Before the consortium bills instrument hours, someone has to certify you're not about to lose the rover to weather nobody forecasted.
missions.briefing: Mars does not issue advisories. It issues consequences. REMS is the minimum viable excuse for saying we checked — pressure, wind, temperature, UV. Activate the station and keep the stream live. Without it, downstream checkout is blocked by policy, not physics. If that feels arbitrary, welcome to the Consortium. The dust doesn't read the handbook either.

missions.name: Power Systems — Shakedown
missions.description: Verify the MMRTG and the emergency thermal dump. Paperwork calls it "standard"; the bus calls it a bad idea executed correctly.
missions.briefing:
Your steady-state budget is roughly a hundred watts of decay heat turned into electricity — the whole mission in one hot brick. Collect the calibration hardware from the cache, then run the overdrive test: thermal reserve to bus, cooldown logged. It's on the checklist because someone once didn't do it and we spent six months in review.

Fair warning: while overdrive is dumping thermal reserve to the bus, every instrument goes offline — including comms. No transmitting, no science, no nothing until the cycle completes. Drive around unsupervised for a bit. Enjoy it. Nobody's billing you.

When the graph flatlines, proceed to the marked outcrop. Spoiler: the landing also scratched the RTG. We'll pretend that's a separate ticket.

missions.name: MastCam — Classifier Baseline
missions.description: Survey pass: teach the vision stack what this patch of Mars looks like, three targets minimum per procedure.
missions.briefing: Optics passed bench. The planet hasn't. Run survey mode, tag at least three rocks so we can file a classifier attestation. The floating labels aren't flair — they're chain-of-custody for anything you shoot or drill later. Skip this step and Legal sends a memo. Nobody wants the memo.

missions.name: ChemCam — LIBS Verification
missions.description: Laser spectroscopy checkout. The laser is real; the permission model is mostly lawyers.
missions.briefing: Classifier sign-off received. ChemCam may only engage MastCam-tagged targets — interlock, not suggestion. One LIBS cycle, spectrum to archive, acknowledge the readout. We call it science. Risk calls it controlled arson at millimeter scale. Tomato, tomato, plasma.

missions.name: Drill — Sample + Power Recovery
missions.description: Extract a core, then demonstrate bus recovery. Two line items, one form — efficiency.
missions.briefing: Percussive rotary checkout: obtain a sample. Note for the file — targets already run through MastCam and ChemCam tend to yield richer material; the pipeline isn't superstition, it's statistics someone once put in a footnote. After the bit stops, you'll be low on electrons. Run the RTG shunt procedure — motors stowed, thermal headroom to the bus — and log it. Accounting loves a logged shunt. Engineering tolerates it.

missions.name: APXS — Contact Science
missions.description: Elemental composition via contact pass. Slower than LIBS. More paperwork per joule.
missions.briefing: ChemCam did standoff. APXS does intimacy — sensor to rock, alpha and X-ray, count until the statistics stop arguing. One clean pass satisfies calibration. If you're bored, remember someone in 2187 still has to peer-review this. Be kind to the future.

missions.name: DAN — Subsurface Baseline
missions.description: Neutron survey checkout. The detector doesn't care what it finds — it just counts what bounces back.
missions.briefing:
DAN fires neutrons at the ground and listens for the slow ones. Hydrogen — usually locked in ice or hydrated minerals — thermalises the return. The detector doesn't know what you're hoping for. It counts what comes back and lets statistics argue later.

Pull up the instrument panel and find DAN. Activate it. Drive around — the passive scan runs automatically while wheels are turning. When the return count spikes, hold position and let it integrate. Could be ice. Could be bound water in clay. Could be a statistical ghost that dissolves at higher confidence. That's the instrument working as designed.

Once a signal resolves, review it in the science archive. Whether it confirms subsurface water or fizzles into noise, the procedure is the same. DAN doesn't do wishful thinking and neither should you.

objectives.label: Drive until DAN registers a signal

missions.name: SAM — Lab Checkout
missions.description: Onboard analytical lab: one full experiment cycle. After this, the oven stops being decorative.
missions.briefing: Load a drill sample, select a mode, run to completion. Pyrolysis, wet chemistry, whatever the checklist demands — each mode answers a different committee. One finished run clears SAM for routine ops and gives Discovery something to put in a press release. The science is optional. The logging is not.

missions.name: UHF — First Uplink
missions.description: Data on-vehicle does not exist for grant accounting until Earth acknowledges receipt. Welcome to orbital windows.
missions.briefing: Queue a science package on UHF during a relay pass. Bandwidth is finite; complexity costs time; the orbiter does not wait for your feelings. One confirmed downlink closes pre-ops checkout on our end. After that you're officially not a training scenario — just an underinsured asset on another planet. Congratulations are in the FAQ, appendix C.

missions.name: LGA — Deep Signal
missions.description: Routine diagnostic (official). Expanded spectrum capability (actual). Funding source: "historical outreach" (nobody asked follow-ups).
missions.briefing: There's a module at the far cache — DSN archaeology firmware for the low-gain path. Install per attached instructions. Most of what you'll hear is garbage: calibration ghosts, deprecated handshakes, beeps from programs defunded before your grandparents were born. Archives thinks there might be recoverable mission tape in the noise. Science Directorate marks it zero priority. You are therefore cleared to waste time on it. Bolt it on. Log the install. If you hear singing, that's not in the scope.
